summary: A good and brave man, an old friend of Cicero, commended to P. Sulpicius Rufus, proconsul of Illyricum (Fam. 13.77, 46 BC). He had seen Cicero's runaway slave Dionysius at Narona and was to brief Sulpicius on the matter. Otherwise unknown.

A slave of Cicero who managed his valuable library, stole many books, falsely claimed to have been manumitted, and fled to Illyricum, where he was seen at Narona (Fam. 13.77, 46 BC). Distinct from the several free men named Dionysius in the corpus, including Cicero's learned freedman M. Pomponius Dionysius.

Roman colony and trading town in southern Dalmatia (Illyricum), on the river Naro (modern Neretva). It lay within the command of P. Sulpicius Rufus; Cicero's runaway slave Dionysius was seen there (Fam. 13.77).

A Greek of Sicyon, guest-friend (hospes) and intimate of Cicero, praised for his probity, worth, and liberality toward his guests. Cicero describes him as nearly the foremost man of Achaea and commends him to A. Allienus (Fam. 13.78, 46 BC). Otherwise unknown.

A son of Cicero's intimate friend C. Avianius Flaccus, sharing his father's praenomen Gaius; an intimate of Cicero whom he held uniquely dear. In 46 BC he was in Sicily, and Cicero commends him and his brother Marcus to A. Allienus, proconsul (Fam. 13.79). Distinct from his father (person:c-avianius-flaccus); the shared praenomen makes the two hard to disentangle.

A son of Cicero's intimate friend C. Avianius Flaccus and brother of the younger C. Avianius; an intimate of Cicero. In 46 BC he was with Cicero at Rome, while his brother was in Sicily; Cicero commends both to A. Allienus, proconsul (Fam. 13.79).
